question: How much does India spend on research, and is it enough?

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

plannedCharts.title: The world spends very differently on research
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO · gross R&D spending as a share of GDP · latest available year per country
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single number that frames the whole article: India spends about 0.6% of its economy on research, a fraction of what the leaders spend.

plannedCharts.title: Research pays — the question is who reaps it
plannedCharts.subtitle: Economics literature · midpoints of wide ranges, shown to make a point, not as precise measurements
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Before asking whether India spends enough, it helps to know what research is worth: the measured returns are high.

plannedCharts.title: India's research spending peaked over a decade ago
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO (India, World) · against the 2% of GDP target set in the 2013 STI Policy
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India set itself a 2% target in 2013 and has drifted further from it, not closer.

plannedCharts.title: More rupees, the same thin sliver
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · gross expenditure on R&D, current prices · fiscal years, latest 2020-21
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: In rupees, India's research budget keeps climbing; the catch is that the economy grew just as fast.

plannedCharts.title: In India, the government still does the research
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · who performs India's R&D · 2020-21
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: In most advanced economies business does most of the research; in India the state still dominates.

plannedCharts.title: Everywhere else, business pays for research
plannedCharts.unit: % of R&D from business / private sector
plannedCharts.subtitle: OECD / WIPO / NITI Aayog · business-enterprise share of R&D · India is its private-industry share (DST 2020-21)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The clearest single diagnosis of India's R&D gap: its private sector barely shows up.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's public research money goes
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · central-government R&D by agency · 2020-21
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The public money that does exist is tilted heavily toward defence, space and atomic energy.

plannedCharts.title: China and Korea pulled away
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO · R&D spending as a share of GDP, India vs the risers
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Two generations ago India and China spent similar shares on research; then their paths split.

plannedCharts.title: India has strikingly few researchers
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO · full-time-equivalent researchers per million population · latest available per country
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Money is only half of it; the other half is people, and India has remarkably few researchers for its size.

plannedCharts.title: India is adding researchers, but from a very low base
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · researchers per million population in India · selected years
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The trend is up, which is real, but the starting point is so low that the climb barely registers globally.

plannedCharts.title: The absolute scale of the gap
plannedCharts.subtitle: OECD MSTI (China, US · 2024) and DST / UNESCO (India · 2020-21) · purchasing-power-parity dollars
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Shares of GDP understate the gap; in absolute money the distance is an order of magnitude.

plannedCharts.title: And yet: third in the world for research papers
plannedCharts.subtitle: NSF Science & Engineering Indicators / Scopus · scientific & technical publications · 2022
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Here is the paradox: India spends like an also-ran but produces like a heavyweight.

plannedCharts.title: Indians now file most of their own patents
plannedCharts.subtitle: WIPO / World Bank · applications filed at the Indian Patent Office, by applicant
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A genuine shift: domestic inventors have gone from a minority of patent filings to a clear majority.

plannedCharts.title: India spends like 50th, innovates like 30th
plannedCharts.subtitle: WIPO Global Innovation Index 2025 · India's rank on innovation inputs vs outputs
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The paradox in one chart: India converts modest inputs into outsized innovation output.

plannedCharts.title: India does science cheap: the ISRO model
plannedCharts.subtitle: Mission budgets · India's space programme vs comparable foreign missions · approximate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The best of Indian research is mission-focused and frugal, doing for tens of millions what others spend hundreds on.

plannedCharts.title: The research money that doesn't get spent
plannedCharts.subtitle: Open Budgets India · share of the budgeted R&D allocation actually used
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Some of India's R&D problem is not too little money but money that never gets out the door.

plannedCharts.title: The new bets on Indian research
plannedCharts.subtitle: ANRF / RDI Scheme · the headline new funding commitments
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's answer is a set of large new vehicles, betting that private money will finally show up.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
